steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The text argues that no single factor fully explains pedestrian habits in Vancouver — demographics, commute distance, and amenities all play a role. The correct choice must reflect that pedestrian amenities are just one of several contributing factors, not the dominant one.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It may increase walkability in Vancouver but is known to reduce walkability in other cities.’: the text never claims amenities harm other cities' walkability; this is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘It is better understood as an effect of the high level of pedestrian traffic in Vancouver than as a cause of that pedestrian traffic.’: reverses the causal direction the text implies. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘It affects walking decisions in Vancouver less than demographic characteristics and average commuting distance do.’: the text explicitly says no single factor dominates, so ranking any factor first contradicts the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘It should be understood as just one of several factors that influence pedestrian activity in Vancouver.’: matches the text's core point that multiple factors each contribute and no one element transfers walkability alone. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
